record.statement: Let G be a finite group of order p where p is prime.
By Lagrange's theorem, the order of any subgroup H of G divides the order of G, so |H| divides p. Hence |H| is either 1 or p.
For any element g in G, the cyclic subgroup ⟨g⟩ generated by g has order equal to the order of g, which must divide p. So the order of any non-identity element is either 1 (identity) or p.
If there exists an element g ≠ e in G, then |⟨g⟩| = p, so ⟨g⟩ = G, making G cyclic.

The key claim reduces to: a nontrivial finite group of prime order must have at least one non-identity element. This holds because |G| = p ≥ 2 (p is prime, so p ≥ 2), so G contains at least the identity and one other element.
